narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 40% chance of failure for you—high enough that it led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was in the bottom tier compared to the typical student's mid-range standing, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. On a positive note, your LSAT score, above the average for applicants, had the strongest counterbalancing influence, nudging the prediction toward success and highlighting one of your standout achievements. Your undergraduate GPA, a bit below the dataset average, also leaned slightly in favor of passing, while your Year 1 decile in the lower ranks added a small push toward failure—but far less than your later performance. Being a full-time student, like most applicants, provided a minor positive tilt, and your family income level helped too by aligning with factors linked to better outcomes. Overall, your Year 3 grades stood out as the biggest concern relative to peers who typically rank higher and pass the bar more reliably, overshadowing your LSAT strength. While this is disappointing, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in academic pipelines, it doesn't define your potential. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your decile rankings through targeted academic support or retakes, perhaps leveraging your LSAT success in appeals or other programs. Consider bridge courses or bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students to build a stronger case next time. You're clearly capable—keep pushing forward, and we're rooting for your success in law.
